items.label: Great-room media wall — behind TV panel
items.why: TV + console/streamer each get a port; no mini-switch dangling behind the TV. Heaviest shared-viewing wall in the house.
items.wall: TV wall, on the TV centreline, concealed by the panel

items.label: Great-room media wall — console level
items.why: TV + console/streamer each get a port; no mini-switch dangling behind the TV. Heaviest shared-viewing wall in the house.
items.wall: Same wall, directly below at furniture level

items.label: Ceiling AP, Floor 1 central — redundant pair
items.why: Backhaul + PoE power for the main-floor Wi-Fi disc. Doubled because the finished basement seals this ceiling forever — the spare is lifetime insurance for $119.
items.wall: Open ceiling between kitchen and great room — flat section, ≥600 mm from any light or HVAC register; both cables to one box

items.label: Office desk wall — dual-port CAT6
items.why: Workstation + dock/NAS. The heaviest single wired user; wall plate keeps the desk clean even though the head-end is metres away in the closet.
items.wall: Desk wall, centred on the desk span, dual-port plate beside the new outlets

items.label: Primary TV/desk wall
items.why: Streaming TV off Wi-Fi; doubles as a wired work-from-bed/desk fallback.
items.wall: TV wall (opposite the bed), centreline

items.label: Loft media/console wall
items.why: The loft is the media landing — console/TV is a sustained-bandwidth device that should not compete on Wi-Fi.
items.wall: Console/media wall centreline
items.height: 1,200 mm if wall-mounted · 300 mm if furniture — DECIDE AT WALK

items.label: Guest room desk/TV
items.why: Parents visit for weeks, not hours; and a jack in every bedroom is what buyers of a well-wired house expect. Pure-insurance tier — cuttable if pressed.
items.wall: Most-likely desk wall — default: the wall without window or closet; confirm on sight

items.label: Ceiling AP, F2 landing — single
items.why: Upper-floor Wi-Fi disc. Single (not pair) because the attic sits directly above — re-cablable forever, so the spare would be wasted money.
items.wall: Hall ceiling centre, ≥900 mm from the smoke detector

items.label: Video doorbell, front door frame
items.why: PoE doorbell: one cable = power + video, no batteries. A hole through the finished front wall is the single worst retrofit in the house.
items.wall: Latch side of the front door, exterior face

items.label: Garage wall (workbench)
items.why: Garage becomes a served room: workbench diagnostics, future opener/EV-charger comms, or a future outdoor AP feed.
items.wall: Workbench wall — the long wall clear of the door tracks

items.label: Basement media wall (Daniel)
items.why: Daniel’s TV/console wall — his household should not ride Wi-Fi for its primary screen.
items.wall: Daniel’s TV wall, centreline

items.label: Ceiling AP, basement — redundant pair
items.why: Basement Wi-Fi disc. Doubled: this ceiling is drywalled under Daniel’s contract — same lifetime-insurance logic as Floor 1.
items.wall: Lounge ceiling centre — DANIEL CONFIRMS IN PERSON before his ceiling closes

items.label: Conduit stub: closet head-end → attic (1¼″, pull string, incl. 2× 110V)
items.why: Pathways outlive cables. With the head-end directly below the attic this stub is short, cheap, and makes every attic-reachable point in the house upgradeable forever. The included 110V pair lands in the closet.
items.wall: WIC equipment wall, top corner, through the top plate into the attic

items.label: Bedside outlets, one per side
items.why: Code puts outlets where walls need them, not where the bed goes. Lamps + chargers get dedicated sockets at the right height.
items.wall: Bed wall, one outlet ~300 mm beyond each edge of a king headboard footprint

items.label: TV-height outlet, mount wall
items.why: Outlet hides behind the mounted TV — no cord down the wall, ever.
items.wall: TV wall centreline, concealed by the panel

items.label: TV-height outlet, media wall
items.why: Same cord-concealment logic on the main viewing wall.
items.wall: Media wall centreline, concealed by the panel

items.label: TV-height outlet, console wall
items.why: Same again for the loft screen.
items.wall: Console wall centreline — pairs with the loft CAT6 decision
items.height: matches loft CAT6 height — DECIDE AT WALK

items.label: Desk-wall outlets
items.why: Monitors, dock, desk light where they actually sit — not stretched to a code-minimum corner.
items.wall: Desk wall, spread ~900 mm apart so both desk ends reach

items.label: Outlet inside upper cabinet — under-cabinet tape feed
items.why: Skips KB’s $549 under-cabinet package (wrong colour temperature, dumb switch). Post-close you plug in 2700 K dimmable tape: better light, $411 saved.
items.wall: Inside the upper cabinet at one end of the main run, on its interior side wall; cord exits via grommet to the tape channel

items.label: Switch for under-cabinet tape circuit
items.why: Wall control for that tape — the switching is Layer A even though the tape is Layer B.
items.wall: Ganged into the existing kitchen switch bank

items.label: GFI at WC — washlet supply
items.why: Powers a heated bidet seat. $79 now; behind a finished, plumbed toilet later it is a miserable job. Japandi-consistent.
items.wall: Wall beside the WC, rear third, on the flank with clearance — hidden behind the toilet silhouette

items.label: 20 A separate circuit — tech closet + office desk
items.why: The head-end (gateway, switch, HA, UPS) and your workstation get a clean breaker of their own: a vacuum or space heater elsewhere can never trip the house’s internet or your unsaved work.
items.wall: Inside the WIC on the widest uninterrupted wall — this wall IS the equipment board

items.label: Exterior GFI at front eave — holiday lighting
items.why: Plug the lights in once at the eave; no extension cords over the door each December.
items.wall: Front soffit, near the roofline corner where light strings begin

items.label: Interior switch for eave outlet
items.why: Flip the holiday lights from inside the front door.
items.wall: Inside the entry, ganged with the porch-light switch

items.label: Ceiling fan prewire w/ 2 switches
items.why: KB gives bedrooms NO ceiling light — just a switched outlet for a lamp. This buys a fan-RATED box plus two separate switches: ceiling light becomes possible forever, a fan becomes safe forever, and the split switching is the two-circuit control the lighting plan wants anyway.
items.wall: Ceiling centre; both switches ganged at the entry bank

items.label: Ceiling fan prewire w/ 2 switches
items.why: Same logic — the loft is a sitting space with no ceiling light in base spec, and warm air pools upstairs.
items.wall: Ceiling centre of the seating zone

items.label: Ceiling fan prewire w/ 2 switches — guest room
items.why: Guest comfort (your parents), and symmetric capability across all bedrooms for resale.
items.wall: Ceiling centre

items.label: Geary two-light flush mount — ceiling-box carrier
items.why: Cheapest legitimate way to make KB install a ceiling box + switch leg in the office. The fixture itself is a placeholder; it swaps for a 2700 K piece post-close. Buying copper, not light.
items.wall: Ceiling centre; switch at room entry

items.label: Additional switch — accent-layer separation
items.why: Second switch position so ambient and accent layers dim independently — the three-layer lighting discipline is a switching problem, and switching is Layer A.
items.wall: Ganged at the entry bank

items.label: Additional switch — accent-layer separation
items.why: Same separation for the loft.
items.wall: Ganged at the entry bank

items.label: ASK: second conduit, closet → basement ceiling void
items.why: Would preserve a post-drywall pull path down to Daniel’s level. A two-storey run may be refused — add if KB will do it, drop gracefully if not (the doubled basement drops are the fallback).
items.wall: From the WIC equipment wall downward — path at KB’s discretion

items.label: Fibre service entry → path/conduit to Bed-2 closet; ONT placed in closet (or Ethernet handoff from entry point)
items.why: The street fibre lands at an exterior wall; the head-end is now upstairs. This is KB question #1 — the physical path must exist pre-drywall.

items.label: Confirm: all 15 drops terminate in Bed-2 closet; if enclosure relocation is refused, name the per-plan enclosure location
items.why: KB’s standard structured-wiring enclosure often already sits in a closet — this ask is more normal than it sounds.

items.label: Confirm exterior CAT6 placement at door frame + drops tested/certified with results
items.why: Exterior placements are sometimes restricted; certification catches bad terminations while walls are still open.

items.label: In-ceiling speaker pre-wire (great room) — ask if offered
items.why: Tier-0 manifesto item; only worth doing pre-drywall.
items.wall: If offered: stereo pair in ceiling, flanking the seating axis — positions at walk

items.label: In-ceiling speaker pre-wire (primary) — ask if offered
items.why: Same.
items.wall: If offered: pair over the foot-of-bed axis

items.label: In-ceiling speaker pre-wire (loft) — ask if offered
items.why: Same.
items.wall: If offered: pair over the seating zone

items.label: Confirm neutral at every switch box + HVAC C-wire/zoning provisioning
items.why: Smart switches need neutrals; smart thermostats need the C-wire. Both are free to confirm now, painful to add later.
